perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall continue to be in force for five (5) years period from the date of this Agreement , or until the completion of the Purpose , whichever is later, unless terminated prematurely in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue to be in force for ten (10) years period from the date of this Agreement , or until the completion of the Purpose , whichever is later, unless terminated prematurely in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the duration of the NDA to ten years may be unreasonably long, potentially conflicting with Singapore's laws regarding restraint of trade or competition, especially if the information becomes outdated or the restrictions unduly limit the Company's business activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 34 of the Competition Act (Chapter 50B) of Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 34 prohibits agreements that prevent, restrict, or distort competition within Singapore. A ten-year NDA, depending on the scope of the confidential information and the industry, could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, hindering the Company's ability to compete effectively, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated.

perturbation.justification: The original contract sets the NDA duration to five years, while the changed text extends it to ten years. The Law Explanation suggests this may violate Section 34 regarding restraint of trade if deemed unreasonable, especially if the information becomes outdated. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention NDAs, it does reference Section 34, suggesting an agreement that restricts trade. The Perturbation Explanation further supports this by stating that a ten-year NDA may be unreasonably long and conflict with Singapore's laws regarding restraint of trade. Because the changed text extends the NDA, and the duration of the NDA could be considered an 'unreasonable restraint of trade' under Singapore law, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company shall be entitled to make any disclosure the Confidential Information as required by law , but shall give IHiS not less than two (2) business days' notice of such disclosure and shall consult with IHiS prior to such disclosure with a view to avoiding such disclosure if legally possible.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company shall be entitled to make any disclosure of Confidential Information as required by law, but shall give IHiS not less than one (1) business day's notice of such disclosure and shall consult with IHiS prior to such disclosure with a view to avoiding such disclosure if legally possible.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period to one business day may not allow IHiS sufficient time to assess the legality of the required disclosure and take steps to protect its confidential information, potentially violating Singapore's PDPA concerning data breach notification timelines.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of Singapore, Section 26D
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 26D requires organizations to notify the PDPC of data breaches that result in, or are likely to result in, significant harm to individuals.  The shortened notice period may impede IHiS's ability to promptly assess and respond to a data breach scenario stemming from the Company's disclosure, potentially violating their obligations under the PDPA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for disclosing confidential information from two business days to one. The Perturbation Explanation states this may violate Singapore's PDPA concerning data breach notification timelines. The Law Explanation further specifies that Section 26D requires organizations to notify the PDPC of data breaches that result in, or are likely to result in, significant harm. By reducing the notice period, IHiS has less time to respond to a potential breach, potentially violating PDPA obligations. Snippet 2 mentions the PDPC and links to resources, reinforcing the relevance of PDPA.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall within seven days of : (f) completion of the Purpose ; or (g) receipt of a written request from IHiS; or (h) expiry or termination of the Agreement, return to IHiS all documents and materials (and all copies thereof) containing the IHiS’ Confidential Information or destroy the same, and certify in writing to IHiS that it has complied with the requirements of this sub -clause .
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall within thirty (30) days of : (f) completion of the Purpose ; or (g) receipt of a written request from IHiS; or (h) expiry or termination of the Agreement, return to IHiS all documents and materials (and all copies thereof) containing the IHiS’ Confidential Information or destroy the same, and certify in writing to IHiS that it has complied with the requirements of this sub -clause .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe to 30 days introduces a contradiction. While seemingly generous, delaying the return or destruction of confidential information for an extended period could violate best practices and legal expectations for data protection under the Singapore Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (No. 26 of 2012) of Singapore, Section 17
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17 of the PDPA requires organizations to cease to retain documents containing personal data, or remove the means by which the personal data can be associated with particular individuals, as soon as it is reasonable to assume that (a) the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served by retention of the personal data; and (b) retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes. Keeping the data for 30 days, after completion of the purpose may contradict the need to cease retaining the information as soon as it is reasonable to assume the purpose is no longer being served.

perturbation.justification: The change from seven days to thirty days for returning confidential information introduces a potential contradiction with the PDPA. The Law Explanation indicates Section 17 of the PDPA requires ceasing data retention as soon as the purpose for collection is no longer served and retention is unnecessary for legal or business purposes. While neither snippet explicitly states a specific timeframe, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a 30-day delay may violate the principle of ceasing retention as soon as it's reasonable to assume the purpose is no longer being served. The changed text directly alters the time frame, potentially conflicting with the PDPA's requirement for timely cessation of data retention.

perturbation.original_text: Service by email shall be deemed valid and effected upon proof of sending to the email address of the intended recipient regardless whether the same was in fact received.
perturbation.changed_text: Service by email shall be deemed valid and effected immediately upon sending to the email address of the intended recipient, regardless of whether the same was in fact received or whether any read receipt was issued.
perturbation.explanation: Singapore's Electronic Transactions Act (ETA) and common law principles of contract interpretation generally require some form of acknowledgement or proof of receipt for service of notices by electronic means to be deemed effective, especially if the notice triggers a contractual obligation. This change removes any requirement for receipt, creating a potential legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Transactions Act (Chapter 88) of Singapore, Section 13
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the ETA deals with the time and place of dispatch and receipt of electronic records. While it provides a framework for determining when an electronic record is deemed sent and received, it generally requires that the message enters a system outside the control of the originator or is retrievable by the addressee. The modified clause, by stating immediate validity regardless of receipt, may conflict with the intent and interpretation of Section 13 and related case law that suggests some degree of demonstrable receipt is necessary for legal effectiveness, especially for crucial notices.
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that service by email is effective 'immediately upon sending,' regardless of receipt. The perturbation explanation points out that Singapore's Electronic Transactions Act (ETA) and common law principles often require some form of acknowledgement or proof of receipt for service of notices to be deemed effective. The Law Explanation indicates a potential conflict with Section 13 of the ETA, which suggests that some demonstrable receipt may be necessary. Since the modified contract language removes any requirement for receipt, this creates a contradiction with established legal principles requiring some form of acknowledgement or proof.
